discussion_comments.comment_body: FYI this has been optimized down to the level of minimizing property accesses in the fast path. There are a lot of unneeded changes happening here. Make it as minimal as possible and it'll be easier for me to benchmark the performance impact.
I removed a bunch of changes here and pushed a new version that I think keeps things minimal

It's problematic that this is eagerly attempted. It'll throw a warning/error in various environments. Zod currently avoids ever using the function constructor if `z.config({ jitless: true })` has been set to avoid spamming users with warnings in these environments. You can still attempt this during module initialization but only when you have high confidence (based on `navigator` that the code is executing in Cloudflare. 

Though more broadly—as I understand it `new Function()` is only allowed during module initialization, so this doesn't actually help. Zod uses `new Function()` inside `getFastPass` when an object schema is parsing for the first tiem. At that point we're out of module initialization so Zod won't be able to properly JIT] this anyway. Your benchmark should include an object parse operation - it would reveal this.

Thinking about this more—there's no guarantee that a Zod schema is defined top-level either so getting Zod JIT to work is just not really possible in the general case until `new Function` can be enabled throughout execution. 

Let me know if I'm missing something. Maybe anonrig has thoughts.
thanks, will take that on and have a think :)

>Though more broadly—as I understand it new Function() is only allowed during module initialization, so this doesn't actually help. Zod uses new Function() inside getFastPass when an object schema is parsing for the first tiem. At that point we're out of module initialization so Zod won't be able to properly JIT] this anyway. Your benchmark should include an object parse operation - it would reveal this.

ahhh this is rough. thanks, explains Sunil's comment.
closing this :)

> new Function() is only allowed during module initialization, so this doesn't actually help. Zod uses new Function() inside getFastPass when an object schema is parsing for the first tiem.

But you can parse the schema for the first time at this point too. It's true that many ppl aren't doing this right now – but they could if they wanted the fast path.

The problem is I don't believe there's currently *any* way to get `fastEnabled` to be true with Zod on Workers because the current check is too coarse (ie "if on Workers, *never* allow JIT.") – even though it would actually work (eg, parse a dummy object during global instantiation to warm up the JIT). This also means that any future changes to functionality on Workers (eg allowing `new Function`) is hampered.

TBH feature detection feels like the right way to go here (allows for `new Function` to work on Workers if this is enabled in the future, and also takes care of any other environment where `new Function` may be disallowed). But if you prefer, it could be only checked if `globalThis.Cloudflare?.compatibilityFlags?.allow_eval_during_startup` is true, and that way not slow down / interfere with any other environment – and also massively reduce the risk of changing any existing behaviour on other platforms.

I used an IIFE with double loop for execution complexity optimisation, this way I'm able to stop the processing as soon as I find a duplicates avoiding to check the rest of the array if it's not necessary. Using A Set would mean to check duplicates for all elements of the array then check the lenght of the Set to get the result. The code would be simpler but the execution would be worse.
Did you test performance of double loop over a Set so you say it's more optimized?
@ArthurKa @Balastrong I mark this thread as resolved as I didn't had any more news from you on that subject after my trade off and implementing the result of the trade off
Avoid throwing errors in hot path for performance reasons

Only instantiating the `ctx` when we need it is actually a performance optimization. Can you move this back into the conditionals and add them to the check conditionals as close to `addIssueToContext` as possible?
Sure things! I didn't know the implication, now I took as example `ZodString` and pushed, thanks :)
`ZodError` has to extend `Error` in order to preserve stack trace information.
...also so it can be caught by users naively using `instanceof Error`.

This is the core performance issue. Zod overuses try/catch in flow. Getting stack information is costly. Removing `Error` is quick fix. I guess the best way would be to change flow so it wouldn't be overused

>I guess the best way would be to change flow so it wouldn't be overused

I fully agree with you. And in fact it looks like your other changes are going a long way in that direction?

Well I tried to remove as many error creations as I could. 
Performance issues in zod are really making me worried. Even our requests where we validate data goes up from 300ms to 1600ms.

I share your concern. We use Zod in production too and if possible we would like to drive its performance up drastically, at least in the happy path. However, removing the inheritance of `Error` would be a breaking chance that I doubt would be palatable to @colinhacks, never mind the users.

In lieu of this change, there are a few things we could do to ensure that `ZodError` is instantiated as rarely as possible. You've already done some of these things in this PR, which I'm thankful for.

My intuition is that the next step after merging your changes to `src/parser.ts` should be to run benchmarks and look at flame graphs, but this isn't something I have much experience with, nor the time to perform right now. Help is always appreciated. While we don't have benchmarks we can also look at making non-API-breaking optimizations.

I zoomed out and saw that you had benchmarks above, which is wonderful! Would you be able to run another benchmark with just your changes to `src/parser.ts`? And can you comment on the composition of the run, if it's mostly successes or failures?

That what I was doing at first. As it does help with types where there are no unions. It does almost nothing where they are. Every union fail creates and throws an error :/ 
That's why my benchmarks only improved when I got rid of `Error`

Oh wow, I didn't realize that was the problem. indeed, union parsing should collect issues without instantiating `Error`.

Let's give @colinhacks a few days to chime in in case there's a particular approach he'd like to take. I understand he's busy with work at the moment.

Was there a performance reason to remove the `parsedType` and `data` from the context? Not a big deal, just caused some extra churn in the updates to the code and wanted to make sure that was intentional and necessary.

Yes, the reason was purely because of performance: adding `parsedType` and `data` required a shallow clone for every level and also accessing them through the object property which had (a slight) penalty hit compared to passing them as separate parameters. Of course there is still the `path` that changes quite frequently so if we want to maximize the performance, that should be passed as a parameter as well.
Seems fine to leave that as a future optimization since it wouldn't affect the public API of the library.
This looks fairly expensive, but I see `Map` is done the same way. @lo1tuma was there any particular reason for the copy?
